[IMAGE:0]It's getting toward midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The picture?  I'm working my way through photos of my lineage, focusing on the teachers whose work has influenced me and the teachers who influenced them in turn. At this point I've finally gone as far back into my Rosicrucian lineage as I can without repeating earlier images -- the other great source of Dion Fortune's lineage, of course, is the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, and its founders and major figures have already been honored here. So it's time to leap back to square one and start talking about the other end of my Druid lineage -- the one that goes through the Order of Bards Ovates and Druids (OBOD). That was the first Druid order I joined, and the source of much of my early Druid training -- and it exists at all because of Philip Carr-Gomm, this week's honoree.

[IMAGE:1]Philip was a young man on the fringes of the London occult scene when he began studying with Ross Nichols, whom we'll discuss in an upcoming post. (That's Philip with the colossal hair in the photo on the right, being initiated into OBOD on top of Glastonbury Tor.) Years later, after Nichols' death, he made the same mistake I did, asked the surviving older members about reviving the order, and ended up being talked into becoming its Chosen Chief.  He proceeded to launch an international correspondence course, write a series of first-rate books on Druidry, and in the process turned OBOD into the world's largest Druid order. He retired from the office of Chosen Chief in 2020, and has been writing books on other topics since then.  Did I imitate him when I became Grand Archdruid of AODA? Whenever I thought I could get away with it, you bet, and it inevitably worked.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

comments.title: Woden's Three Deals and Question on Nine Thrall Mowers
comments.content:
Good Evening,

A happy belated Earth Day to everyone!

To Share: I'm trying to work out a seeming parallel between the tales of Woden giving his eye for a drink of the Well of Memory, sacrificing himself to himself to gain the Runes, and getting the Mead of Poetry, and I wrote this post to see if I could get anywhere with it: https://jpowellrussell.com/#woden_s_three_deals

To Ask: I asked last week, but it was rather late in the day, so I'll try again here with a bit more time to breath, for JMG and/or any of the commentariat. On his way to win the Mead of Poetry, Odin runs into nine thralls (slaves) mowing grass for hay with scythes. He takes out a whetstone and sharpens the scythes. All of the thralls want the whetstone and offer to buy it, but instead he throws it in the air, they all clamor after it, and in the ruckus, slit each other's throats. Having created a job opening, Odin goes on to do the work of nine men all summer long for the ettin Baugi, in exchange for his help trying to get a drink of the Mead of Poetry, which is held by Baugi's brother, Suttung. I've meditated on this part of the tale before, but it seems like there might be some allusion (astronomical?) or symbolic implication I'm not getting, so if anyone has any thoughts on where I might look for insight, or ways of thinking about the story, I'd very much appreciate it.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

Now, Saturn being linked with Odin would be a bit of a poser, even if that association is right, but certainly some good food for meditation.

Thank you!

Perhaps though, the astrological metaphor could be just a positioning in time of the events, without direct correlation/association with the pertinent Gods. "Meditation fodder this is." -Yoda

replies.title: Re: Woden's Three Deals and Question on Nine Thrall Mowers
replies.content:
I can find very little information on Norse numerology. Nine was an important symbolic number: the Nine Realms, Odin hanging nine days and nights on Yggdrasil, Heimdall and his nine mothers. The nine thralls may just be a reference to human sacrifice. Given we have just scraps of Norse myth filtered through the pens of Christian redacters and that with no overarching standardized myths, we have only story variants depending on which group of myths you read, it's nearly impossible to know for certain what any of this signified to the Norse themselves. You may have to settle for reinventing the wheel as regards to meaning.

Your article about the whetstone brought an interesting image to mind. Meteors falling to earth would have been a startling sight. If they were iron meteors that would have been a bonanza to smiths seeking iron to work with. Ancient Egyptians used meteoric iron, calling it the 'metal of Heaven'. Norse smith were no doubt aware of the otherworldly origin of this iron and had their own myths, some of it tying into the whetstone stories. Just a thought.

JLfromNH/Aquamarine Addlepated Mongoose

replies.title: Re: Woden's Three Deals and Question on Nine Thrall Mowers
replies.content: Thanks for this! I haven't found a link between whetstones and meteors just yet, but it's certainly worth thinking about, if nothing else. That might also be a link with smith stories. I can't remember off the top of my head if any of the famous swords of Norse lore are made with meteoric iron, but it wouldn't surprise.

replies.title: Re: Woden's Three Deals and Question on Nine Thrall Mowers
replies.content:
Thinking of the connection between Odin and Hermes, Odin would perhaps also be linked with negotiation and profit. I certainly look at his leadership and see very little direct use of force or power, his capabilities seem more linked to perception, communication, decision, cleverness, and coming out of deals with more than he invested.

On this last note, if you view each of his transactions as profitable, it is instructive on relative values. True and abiding wisdom is worth more than enduring physical harm and the loss of physical sight (or at least the loss of depth perception.) The Runes, the communication and magic they are capable of, are worth suffering and death - literally, a piece of writing can outlive many writers, so a single life traded for the power of runes is a bargain.

Thinking of the final exchange upon which you ruminate, Odin offers the thralls technology that will ease their labor and increase their comfort - which is then shown to be a fool's bargain that leads to death by that very technology. (Perhaps an unpleasant indicator of our current predicament.) Then Odin himself takes on nine men's labor for a summer, and not only the labor but their subservience to a Giant master, all for the sake of poetry - which is presented as a profitable exchange! So labor should not be avoided, and technology/ease should be looked upon with suspicion and caution. Even subservience to one's enemy should not be disdained. More complexity is introduced with Gunnlodh, her longing and hope are the final key to poetry. After long arduous labor, the aspiring poet meets an eager audience and sates their desires, thereby gaining mastery of poetry. So, poetic inspiration can be found within any labor or situation, perhaps it is even the direct result of such adversity.

Linking all three, perceptive wisdom poetically inscribed is worth loss, death, or -even more intimidatingly- it is worth suffering a harsh life for.

replies.title: Re: Woden's Three Deals and Question on Nine Thrall Mowers
replies.content: Thanks very much for this, I especially like the framing that each of these transactions was profitable, so work out how. I hadn't noticed the juxtaposition of ease/comfort with Odin's labor, that's a worthwhile line of thinking to go down. I also hadn't thought of Gunnlodh in the role of audience - that's also an interesting one. And I like your summary at the end, thank you again for shedding further light on all this!

replies.title: Re: Woden's Three Deals and Question on Nine Thrall Mowers
replies.content:
Glad it was helpful. :)
Now I must thank you! I hadn't put all this together until you asked about it last week, your posts were the impetus for my reading and this train of thought.

comments.content:
Good evening!

I have seen Phillip's Tea with a Druid from time to time, couldn't help but notice how calm and composed he is. Felt kind of envious! When I started doing practices I was hoping to go and find all the secrets of the universe; after meditating for a while and noticing just how much of a mess things are in my head I quickly forgot about that... if I get things in order and get some of that calmness and composure I will call it a win, and maybe a little insight about a thing or two about the universe would be great ^_^

A small question today. I have heard of many philosophers either getting depressed or crazy about too much philosophizing. Do you have advice about how to ponder such things without everything else becoming "meaningless" or going crazy about such or such idea on the other? You personally have studied and written about what I would consider strong and depressing things such as the decline of our civilization and intense spirituality such as magic and occultism but all in all in your podcasts you seem joyful and composed.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Too much focus on any one part of life will mess you over. That's especially true of philosophy, because it's so easy to get caught up in abstractions and lose track of the rest of life. Thus intensive mental concentration needs to be balanced with ordinary physical activities, creative work, interactions with other people, and the rest of life, to keep everything in balance.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you. So it is not so much about keeping something in mind while thinking too much but focusing completely on other aspects. That makes a lot of sense, for the same reason that exercising too much, even if with the right gear and technique will probably invite an injury.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Exactly. Above the entrance to the Delphic Oracle, right next to the more famous Γνῶθι σεαυτόν, Gnothi Seauton "Know Thyself," was written Μηδὲν ἄγαν, Miden Agan, "Nothing in Excess." It's good advice.

comments.title: Cards and Healing
comments.content:
After my Essene Apprentice initiation, I have been having interesting dreams--

Among them, I had one in which I was playing a form of Solitaire with an ordinary deck of cards. The cards were giving me insights into the root problems of people who needed blessing and healing. Once each problem was divined in a three-card group, I would bundle them together with an elastic and the person's name on a paper on top, and then put the card deck outside where the Sun would 'clear' their medical and spiritual problems.
Within the dream, I was doing all this at night in my sleep, and didn't remember anything about it until I could not find any decks of cards IN the house, and found several bundles of weathered and faded cards OUTside of the house.

After awakening, I found a couple of references to using Tarot cards in healing work, like this one:

https://www.goodreads.com/en/book/show/53141180

Are you or any my esteemed co-readers aware of approaches to healing that involve tarot or other cards? I'd be grateful for any leads for continued study and very interested in hearing about the process of it from anyone who has done this, and the results.

Thanks, JMG, for providing a place to learn about these things!

--Tangerine Insipid Marmot

replies.title: Re: Cards and Healing
replies.content: Hmm! No, I haven't heard of this, but it wouldn't surprise me to learn that it's in existence -- and if nobody's invented it yet, why, that may be your job.

replies.title: Re: Cards and Healing
replies.content:
Hi Tangerine Insipid Marmot...

That's some really interesting dreams you have going on. I had some doozies myself, though quite different, after the apprentice attunement.

To answer your question you might be interested in picking up a copy of Josephine McCarthy's book "Magical Healing: A Health Survival Guide for Occultists, Pagans, Healers and Tarot Readers" It has two chapters on using the "Tarot as a healing Tool." She gives several spreads for reading a persons health and examples on interpreting the readings. In the appendix she gives keywords for tarot that relate to health. For example, for the Page of Swords she has "nerve issues, usually localised" and for the Ten of Pentacles "overgrowth of something, coagulation". Five of Wands is fever or inflammation, and Five of Cups is a fluid imbalance, etc. Her system may be of use to you, and could certainly be expanded on.

She has made the book available as a free download here:

https://static1.squarespace.com/static/539af6bee4b0cef061847e36/t/5ee8c501f2f4ab793cf20c2b/1592313097307/Health%2BBook.pdf

...but a hard copy can also be purchased.

The book also has some really interesting information on the use of homeopathic remedies and herbs in a magical way, and how they can be used to keep magicians in balance over the course of their work. She tackles a number of other topics of interest to the occultist, magician, and healer as well.


Justin Patrick Moore / Heliotrope Malodorous Stoat

Dear JMG,

I have a couple of historical questions:

1. The earliest reference I cans find to ‘vibrating’ divine names is in the original Golden Dawn volumes. Is this something they invented or does it have earlier antecedents?

2. Does this also apply to the LBRP?

Many thanks

O.

replies.title: (no subject)

1) As far as I can tell it was a secret technique in some English and French magical circles in the 19th century -- how far back, I don't know. It would still be secret, except that Crowley and Regardie published the details.

comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
Greetings JMG and everyone,

Wanted to ask you a few things today.

- On re-birth:
1) How did you become sufficiently confident that what you were seeing as past lives was real and unlikely the types of delusions that the astral light reflects back at you? (esp since we now know that past life regression therapies are often/mostly fake).

I often wonder this since even Steiner ultimately bought into everything he was seeing and that makes the thing harder for all of us.

2) Since you have access to these memories, have you found character to roughly be similar across lifetimes, or can you be vastly different in each life? I'm wondering from the perspective of the propensity to do good and evil, and how natural that is to each lifetime.

If in a current lifetime you are living well and positively, but have a lot of negative karma coming your way, I wonder how much of that is recent vs lifetimes ago when one's character was less developed/more prone to negative or evil acts.

3) Thus how much of developed character and spirituality becomes the starting point of the next lifetime, and how much do you have to start from scratch each time?

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
1) I still consider my past life memories as likely rather than certainly true; certainty is a bad habit of the human mind. My decision to treat them as more likely true than not came partly because they contain details I could confirm later -- for example, I was able to describe the clothing I wore in one life during the Regency (a period I hadn't studied at all at that time) and had it confirmed by someone far more expert than I am that this was the appropriate clothing for a person of the social class I was in at that time. Another part was the extent to which the memories explain things otherwise baffling about likes and dislikes I had from earliest childhood -- for example, I've always loathed cars, which is not surprising since I died in on last time around. Finally, there's the simple fact that the memories contain nothing absurd; I wasn't Cleopatra, or anything else particularly special, and the vast majority of the memories have nothing that could qualify as wish-fulfillment. But it's still likely, not certain.

2) There's a very definite continuity of character, but as I think back it's not hard to see myself change and grow, resolve one set of problems and blunder into another, try various things to get past some internal barrier, and so on. It's rather like looking back on one life, but of course there's much more complexity.

3) In my experience you pick up at the start of each life where you left off at the end of the life before. I had a lot of unfinished business at the end of my last life, for example, and it made the childhood of this one kind of a mess.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
That makes sense, thank you.

replies.title: to know
replies.content:
Hello, randomactsofkarma:

The verb the quote references as 'to know' is yod-dalet-ayin (that's the root of it; Hebrew works with three letter roots of basic meaning, and conjugates them in all kinds of ways to creatively diversify and inflect the meaning.)

Yod-dalet-ayin has quite a broad sweep of translations. It generally means 'to know', and our English sense of the meaning of the verb to know has been influenced by the King James translation of the bible, which did accurately capture that in certain contexts, yod-dalet-ayin can indicate carnal relations. It can also indicate Divine knowledge. And it's used in treaties. And a whole lot of other places!

But the Bible generally separates love and sexual relations. The word for love would be aleph-hey-waw. And it doesn't mean "to know", but rather is related to the root word for gift.

Just something to think about. I suspect a better original text to analyze for linguistic clues on Christianity and love would be the Gospels. The earliest manuscripts we have of those are in Koine Greek (although it's unclear if the authors originally wrote them in Greek, Hebrew or Aramaic.)

--Ms. Krieger

replies.content:
Okay, let's unpack this a little at a time.

First, one of the things that's odd about the current fixation on pronouns is that you don't use them when talking to the person in question -- only about the person in question. In English our second person pronoun, "you," is genderless; only in the third person do we get into "he," "she," "it," et al. So it's not about being polite to the person, it's about the person trying to control how other people talk about him/her/whatever.

Second, you're quite correct that there's something to male and female that isn't limited to the material body. In occult philosophy it's taught that human beings have multiple bodies, each on its own plane of being; these bodies each have their own gender; and they aren't all the same gender as the material body. In most (though not all) cases, they alternate -- thus a person with a male material body will usually have a female etheric (life energy) body, a male astral (emotional) body, and a female mental sheath, and a person with a female material body will usually have a male etheric body, a female astral body, and a male etheric sheath. (The soul, the center of consciousness that inhabits these bodies, has no gender.)

Third, the "uncanny valley" is a real thing. You get some people whose subtle bodies aren't arranged as described above, and they feel different. That's one of the factors behind "gaydar" and the like; a great many gay men, for example, have female instead of male astral bodies, and if you're at all sensitive, you can perceive that as a difference in feeling.

As for your friend, well, since I have Aspergers syndrome I'm not the right person to ask for advice on how to deal with people!

How can I tell whether the thought forms or images I perceive are created by me or stem from a being that exists independently from myself? Say, for example, I am in the forest and would like to commune with the elemental of a specific plant. Would performing the LBRP be helpful in this regard?

Furthermore, in the context of meditating on past incarnations, how can I determine if the images I experience are genuine reflections of my past lives or merely fabrications of my untrained mind?

Essentially, I am seeking to understand the intersection between subjective and objective reality within the realm of imagination. How can I discern the difference, if any, between these two aspects?

replies.title: Re: Good Morning
replies.content:
That's not an easy thing! Every thought you experience and every image you perceive, whether you think of the thought or image as "yours" or not, is a fusion of material from within your consciousness and material from outside it. This is even true of the ordinary senses -- look at one of those optical illusions that you can see in two different ways, and get it to flip back and forth, and you can get a glimpse of how your consciousness constructs the worl you experience.

That being the case, it takes much practice to learn how to tease apart the internal and external sides of experience. Daily meditation helps a great deal, because that teaches you to recognize the "flavor" of your own consciousness, and thus helps you recognize what isn't from within you.

comments.title: public transport succubi
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

I have a strange story and would appreciate your input on this case.

For work reasons, I travel regularly on long distance trains in Europe.

On one occasion I was already seated, minding my own business, when a woman entered my field of vision and stared into my eyes for an uncomfortably long time - but it was probably only a few milliseconds longer than a normal look, her eyes were a very light brown, and I felt like I was in a zoo looking into the cage of a predator. She wore a gold bee (maybe wasp?) pendant on a chain around her neck, it was made in an archaic style, it looked like something that might have been made in ancient Greece or the ancient Middle East. At that moment, the pendant struck me as very important - I don't usually give much thought to women's jewelry.

After she sat down across from me, I returned my attention to my book, but within a minute or so my heart began to race, I felt somewhat sexually aroused, but did not have an erection. I tried to concentrate on my book and managed to slow my heartbeat and settle down into a somewhat uncomfortable confusion.

At that moment, another woman sitting next to me moved to get something from her bag and brushed against my arm. I had the same feeling that one has in a dream a second before a nocturnal emission, like "oh no, it's coming", at that moment I really ejaculated. With a jump I grabbed my bag and ran from my seat to the bathroom to clean up and put on another pair of pants. I hid in a seat in a corner at the other end of the train.

This experience deeply unsettled me. I spent days banishing several times a day, using every method I could get my hands on to get rid of any malicious magic around me. To me, it was almost certain that the bee woman had something to do with it, perhaps because of a spell or maybe because she was some sort of entity herself.

That was about a year ago, and I haven't had any more accidents like that in the meantime, although I was very, very afraid of it happening again for a time. Perhaps I should add that I am a married man with a regular sex life, have no mental illnesses, and my body is healthy as far as I know.

Now my question: do you have any idea what happened? Are there people who have such a succubus-like effect on their surroundings? Does it have something to do with bee symbolism?

Thank you

replies.title: Re: GSF questions
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's a good one to meditate on, but there's quite a bit of background. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Not really. Pain is pain; all at once or extended over a period doesn't make much difference.

2) No. Your intention has some influence, but that's true of any action. There were several very distinct cultures along the Atlantic seaboard in the 1600s and 1700s, due to the different backgrounds of the settlers in each region. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The Celtic GD is broader in its focus, because Druidry is much more open-ended. The goal of training in the Celtic GC is to have the necessary magical and alchemical skills to pursue your own spiritual development along whatever path seems appropriate to you.

2) The current SRIA is a little more than that. There are two Colleges (local lodges) currently working, one in Florida and one in North Carolina. I understand the caution of the current leadership, though; let rituals like that out into circulation, and in about two weeks you can be sure that somebody's going to have launched a rival organization. I may talk to the Supreme Magus and see if she'd be willing to have me draft some self-initiation rituals based on Plummer's original rites, which I have.

Whether or not anything comes of that, though, you haven't gone off the deep end at all. Self-initiation is always a valid option in occultism; you always have the option of picking up the contacts yourself, using the teachings and traditions you know of, and seeing what you can do with it. You might not want to stay too close to Waite's rituals, though, because those are still in use: his Fellowship of the Rosy Cross was revived some years back, with the help of members of the original order, and is currently active in Britain, the US, and several other countries. That said, you could probably triangulate between Waite's rituals, Regardie's rituals, and anything else Rosicrucian you can find (there's a lot of stuff available in online archives) to assemble a ritual. Just remember to do it more than once!

comments.title: Planetary... something
comments.content:
Hello JMG, I’ve been pondering this question for several weeks now...

About five years ago, I had reached a point where I was completely fed up with the mess I had made of my life; especially with the way that my emotional ups and downs were totally outside my control and essentially hurled me around like a dinghy on a stormy sea.
Trying to find a way to get out of this mess, I looked at my natal chart and decided that, since both my natal Moon and my South Node were in Cancer, the way to remedy my sorry condition was to focus on the North Node, which naturally was located in the opposite sign of Capricorn. So I set out to live my life the Saturnian way, with daily to-do lists and strict self-discipline, and most of all, emotional sobriety. It helped that I was sick and tired of all the overwrought emotional drama in my life, and had already reached a point where I were just so over it.
Over the years, I noticed some funny effects that went way beyond becoming more self-disciplined: I’ve developed (and I have no idea how, because I didn’t do anything about this consciously) an uncanny time sense: whatever time period I set for myself, I intuitively know when it’s over: no matter if I need to tend to something after five minutes or twenty-three, or forty-seven, I always know when the time is up (usually several seconds before the kitchen timer goes off, because I still like to check this against a clock). I also have perfect timing when I multitask. I was notoriously tardy, always late to appointments — this now never happens, I’m always super punctual. None of the above requires any conscious effort, btw.
There are some equally remarkable, but not so funny effects: all my writing has dried up completely, and I cannot remember my dreams anymore (I used to have a vivid dream life!). I have a Moon-Mercury trine in my chart, so this feels like I’ve amputated a vital part of myself! I live a very isolated life, and feel acutely lonely. One of the wackier side-effects is that I developed an allergy against my (plant-based) hair dye, so I’ve been forced to let my hair grow out, which is completely white (I’m not that old yet, no wrinkles, LOL, so the effect is rather striking — you wouldn’t believe the number of women congratulating me a propos of nothing for being so ‘brave’, or the men commenting, also a propos of nothing, on how good it supposedly looks. Complete strangers, I might add.) It seems as if I’m turning into a little Saturn myself here, something that I did NOT intend!

My questions:

1) What exactly have I been doing here? I didn’t do any rituals or invocations, so I hesitate to call it magic, and I also didn’t pray or make offerings, so I hesitate to call it religion, but I did try to... well.. do things in a way that would ‘please Saturn’. And apparently, doing this did have some transformative effects on me.

2) I feel that I created quite a bit of imbalance, and wonder what I could do to correct that a bit. Not just bringing back a bit more Moon energy to revive my writing, but I feel the need to have a bit more Sun and Venus in my life to counteract the loneliness and depression of my Saturnian lifestyle. I’m thinking of going the same route as I did with emulating Saturn, but while I can think of activities for almost all planets, I draw a blank when it comes to the Sun. The only thing I can think of is something like stage performances, but I’d like to have something I can do on a more daily basis. Any suggestions?

3) Would it be a good idea to start doing planetary charity, or reciting the Orphic hymns? Or would that put things into overdrive?

Thank you for providing this space! I’m learning quite a lot here.

replies.title: Re: Planetary... something
replies.content:
1) You invoked Saturn in the most effective way, and got what you invoked. Saturn isn't all that impressed with rituals or offerings; he wants to see you walk your talk.

2) Now that you've got the Saturnine parts of life down pat, you can start balancing Saturn with other planets. To activate the Solar aspects of your personality, you need to become a little more flamboyant and a little more focused on expressing yourself.

3) That's possible, but it can be pretty potent. Are you up for invoking all seven planets, each on its proper day? That way you can keep yourself in balance. The Orphic hymns and seven colored candles will do the trick.

replies.title: Re: Vampires and Werewolves
replies.content:
(The following is presumably not for posting, unless you think that for some reason it's a good idea.)

I don't know if this is something you've noticed yet, but the modern storytelling figure of the werewolf seems to correspond to a carrying-over after Ragnarok, where both Odin and Fenrir got some measure of what they wanted. Fenrir wanted to be famous, and werewolves are one of the like four most famous kinds of supernatural critters in modern storytelling. Odin wanted to survive, and werewolves are at least a thing, and the werewolves of modern stories are hard to kill.

There are other points of correspondence:

- Werewolves are often accounted as being at least sometimes in a state of frenzy, which is associated with Odin.

- Werewolves are often accounted as eating people, even if sometimes only uncommonly. Which is how Odin and Fenrir would have gotten into this situation.

- Werewolves are typically accounted as being resistant or immune to most weapons. Odin's third charm is about making weapons ineffective.

- Odin's seventeenth and especially sixteenth charms suggest that he would like to have a primal allure if that was an option.

- Werewolves are often accounted as being chained or imprisoned for the duration of their frenzy. Often they are accounted as breaking chains. Odin's fourth charm is about escaping chains. In a sense, what is sometimes accounted as the most secure form of chain is intangible, made of the werewolf's own discipline and experience.

The part about the full moon and silver doesn't really have a correspondence unless you count some kind of solar/telluric modus vivendi thing.

I guess it's not impossible that one of the people who contributed to modern werewolf tropes was bringing in pieces of Fenrir's myths, but it seems less probable that they were including Odin's myths consciously.

It wouldn't be hard to mythologize something more concrete around this connection, since Nidhoggr isn't specified as dying in Ragnarok, and it's unclear what eating the dead entails.

(Further, I had a synchronicity around a figure which appears to combine the protectiveness of Thor with the bestial qualities of Jormungandr, though it would be awkward to explain. The synchronicity works best if Jormungandr is thought of as having had a gray or silvery color. The association with Thor was very clear, almost obnoxiously clear. There are some related stories where the corresponding figure seems to end up in more of a story role of achieving their goals by choosing whom to obey, empower, or advise, rather than doing things by themselves, and often is working from a position of being trapped, bound, or in thrall.

I don't mean to say this is especially strong evidence about anything, but I suppose it is nonzero evidence. For me, interpreting these synchronicities is all sort of a "blooming, buzzing confusion", where there's clearly something (e.g. astral light) playing off my own propensities to assign meaningfulness. I generally don't know how to relate to the idea that there could be some consistent deeper things that these synchronicities would be manifestations of. After all, it seems like these kinds of weird semi-meaningful things happen to me almost as often, whether or not there's independent reason to suppose there'd already be some fully-consolidated consistent deeper thing, the way a synchronicity might be naively interpreted as indicating. Maybe it's like how, in a language, the word-associations are mostly arbitrary, but the need for there to be a language is not arbitrary, and word-associations have to be built up over time?)

One reason not to publicize this is because both of them, Fenrir especially, would have been humiliated at the thought of people knowing things had turned out this way. (Last time I was sort of making the mental motion of planning to comment about this in an ordinary public way, there was immediately a small earthquake.)

But it would be quite in character for Norse mythology for Odin to get what he wanted at a price he would have paid, but that involved dramatic alteration and even humiliation, not something shameful as such, but something that people would have certainly gossiped about.

I don't know if the following fact will turn out be relevant, but I wouldn't have noticed this connection if I hadn't been thinking about ways to get past the setup for Ragnarok that were less wastefully omnicidal. Because, um, reasons.
